title: Intro to Queues: The Conveyor Belt
subtitle: Learn how a queue moves data in one direction: in at the back, out at the front.
encouragement: Queues are how the real world organizes fairness. First come, first served. Once you build one with paper, you'll spot queues everywhere, from grocery lines to printer jobs.
realWorldExample: Think of a conveyor belt at a sushi restaurant. New plates are placed at one end and customers grab them from the other end. The first plate placed is the first plate taken. That's a queue.

materials.detail: About 12 inches long, 2 inches wide. This is your conveyor belt.
To draw boxes and label the front and back.
About 1 inch each. Write these on them: J1, J2, J3, J4, J5, J6 (J for "job").
One marks the FRONT (where items leave). The other marks the BACK (where items enter).

setup: Draw 6 equally-spaced boxes on your paper strip. Number them 0 through 5 underneath.
Place one paperclip at index 0 and label it FRONT (this is where items exit).
Place the other paperclip at index 0 and label it BACK (this is where the next item will enter).
Think of the strip as an arrow pointing LEFT. Index 0 is the pointed tip (the exit). Higher indices are toward the wide end (the entrance).
Lay out your job cards (J1 through J6) nearby.

steps.instruction: A queue is a line. Items enter at the back and leave from the front. Think of people joining a line at a store: new arrivals go to the back, the person at the front gets served first.
steps.conceptNote: This pattern is called FIFO: First In, First Out. The first item added is the first item removed. The opposite of a stack's LIFO.
steps.hint: A stack is a Pringles can (one opening). A queue is a tunnel (two openings, one for entry, one for exit).

steps.instruction: Enqueue J1. This means "add J1 to the back of the queue." Place J1 at the BACK pointer.
steps.action: Place J1 in box 0. Move the BACK paperclip to index 1. FRONT stays at index 0.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [J1][ ][ ][ ][ ][ ]. FRONT at 0, BACK at 1. One item in the queue.
steps.conceptNote: "Enqueue" means add to the back. The back pointer always advances to show where the NEXT item will go.

steps.action: Place J2 in box 1 (BACK moves to 2). Place J3 in box 2 (BACK moves to 3). FRONT stays at 0.
steps.conceptNote: Notice: FRONT hasn't moved. New items always go to the back. The front only changes when something is removed.

steps.instruction: Now dequeue. This removes the item at the FRONT. Which item will leave the queue?
steps.action: Remove J1 from box 0. Move the FRONT paperclip to index 1. J1 was served first because it arrived first.
steps.checkpoint: Strip: [ ][J2][J3][ ][ ][ ]. FRONT at 1, BACK at 3. J1 has left the queue.
steps.conceptNote: "Dequeue" means remove from the front. The first item enqueued (J1) was the first one dequeued. That's FIFO.
steps.hint: Compare this to a stack: a stack would have removed J3 (the last item added). A queue removes J1 (the first item added).

steps.instruction: Enqueue J4 and J5. Then dequeue once more.
steps.action: Place J4 in box 3 (BACK to 4). Place J5 in box 4 (BACK to 5). Then dequeue: remove J2 from box 1, move FRONT to index 2.
steps.conceptNote: Items flow through the queue like a conveyor belt. They enter at the back, wait their turn, and exit at the front. J3 is next to be served.

steps.instruction: Look at your strip. Boxes 0 and 1 are empty but wasted. The queue is marching forward and leaving empty space behind. What happens when BACK reaches the end?
steps.action: Think about it: BACK is at 5, only one slot left. After that, the strip runs out even though boxes 0 and 1 are empty!
steps.conceptNote: This is a real problem with simple array-based queues. The solution is a circular queue: when BACK reaches the end, it wraps around to index 0 and reuses the empty space. You'll build this in a future tutorial.
steps.hint: Imagine taping the ends of your strip together into a loop. That's a circular queue.

steps.instruction: Real-world queue: a print queue. You send 3 documents to the printer. Which one prints first?
steps.action: The first one you sent. The printer processes jobs in the order they arrive: FIFO. If you send a 50-page report, then a 1-page memo, the memo waits until the report finishes.
steps.conceptNote: Print queues, task schedulers, message queues between servers, buffering in streaming video, all use FIFO queues. Fairness means first come, first served.

steps.instruction: Summarize a queue's operations on a card. A queue has two main operations and one inspection.
steps.action: Write: enqueue (add to back), dequeue (remove from front), peek (look at front without removing).
steps.checkpoint: Your card should say: enqueue, dequeue, peek. Like a stack, all three are O(1).
steps.conceptNote: Stacks: LIFO, push/pop from the same end. Queues: FIFO, enqueue at the back, dequeue from the front. Two different structures for two different needs.
steps.hint: Ask yourself: does this problem need the most RECENT item (stack) or the OLDEST item (queue)?

reflection.question: In one sentence, what is FIFO?
reflection.answer: The first item added is the first item removed, like a line at a store.

reflection.question: What is the difference between enqueue and dequeue?
reflection.answer: Enqueue adds an item to the back of the queue. Dequeue removes an item from the front. They operate on opposite ends.

reflection.question: How is a queue different from a stack?
reflection.answer: A stack is LIFO (last in, first out) and operates from one end. A queue is FIFO (first in, first out) and operates from two ends: items enter at the back and leave from the front.

reflection.question: Why does a simple array-based queue waste space, and what's the fix?
reflection.answer: As items dequeue from the front, those slots go unused. The queue marches forward until it runs out of room. The fix is a circular queue that wraps around and reuses empty slots at the beginning.
